item.title: Harvard’s grade inflation experiment
item.description: Like monetary inflation, runaway A’s in higher education are a collective-action problem.

item.title: Tinkering with stamp prices won’t save the Postal Service
item.description: The inflation-adjusted price of sending a letter is roughly the same as it was in the late 1970s.

item.title: Dating in the AI age
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on the growing use of artificial intelligence for dating.

item.title: A seismic shift in nuclear energy regulation
item.description: The Nuclear Regulatory Commission has adopted potentially transformative licensing reforms.

item.title: The skunk at the garden party
item.description: A bad inflation report helps explain worsening consumer sentiment.

item.title: D.C.’s economy shrank by 8.3 percent in the fourth quarter
item.description: The latest GDP numbers are a wake-up call for voters picking the next mayor.

item.title: Should parents be punished for their children’s crimes?
item.description: Gun violence, free speech and cross-dressing, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: AI paranoia becomes unhinged
item.description:
An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.
00:00 - Monologue 03:03 - AI paranoia becomes unhinged 13:14 - Trump threatens big pharma 20:10 - NFL draft forces school closures?
The Washington Post is owned by Jeff Bezos, who founded Amazon.

item.title: This simple penalty would do wonders for democracy
item.description: Reconstruction, birthright citizenship and Trump’s decor, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: What Anthropic’s new nightmare means, in plain English
item.description: Anthropic shared with the world what it learned from its breakthrough model. Would China?

item.title: U.S. missile burn rate in Iran leaves the Pacific cupboard bare
item.description: China is paying attention as America depletes its stockpile.

item.title: A new poll shows the political risk to Israel from the war in Iran
item.description: Launching the attack was a big gamble with American opinion that isn’t paying off.

item.title: Trump’s outrageous Hormuz talk shuns basic strategy
item.description: U.S. power rests on open seas.

item.title: Why Catholic converts are surging with an unexpected demographic
item.description: For a growing segment of young people, religion is rebellion.

item.title: An unpardonable abuse of presidential power with only one solution
item.description: The “power to grant reprieves and pardons” has become another source of political brutishness.

item.title: Iran is trying to turn off America’s water
item.description: Many water facilities lack basic cybersecurity measures, making them vulnerable to adversaries.

item.title: I'm not anti-tax. But this one should go.
item.description: Host Megan McArdle breaks down why the corporate tax system is so complex, costly and potentially inefficient — and explores a bold idea: What if we eliminated it altogether? From hidden economic trade-offs to who really pays corporate taxes, this episode challenges how we think about fairness, efficiency and the future of taxation.

item.title: Rerun
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on Trump, Iran war, ceasefire.
